name: Pontianak
description: The Pontianak is a vengeful female spirit in Malaysian folklore, believed to be the ghost of a woman who died during childbirth. Her name translates roughly to 'stillborn child', reflecting her tragic origins. She is often depicted as a beautiful woman with long black hair, pale skin, and sharp fingernails. However, this alluring facade hides a terrifying creature capable of great violence. The Pontianak typically preys on men, especially those who disrespect women or are traveling alone. She is said to lure victims with her sweet voice and the scent of frangipani flowers, before revealing her true, horrifying form and attacking them. Her presence is often foreshadowed by the sound of a baby crying or the presence of a strong floral scent. The Pontianak is a potent symbol of the dangers faced by women in childbirth and the consequences of disrespecting the feminine. She is a powerful and feared figure in Malaysian culture, serving as a cautionary tale and a reminder of the importance of respecting traditional beliefs.
subtitle: Vengeful spirit of childbirth death
appearance: The Pontianak is typically described as a beautiful woman with long, flowing black hair that conceals her face. She has pale skin and piercing red eyes. Her fingernails are long and sharp, capable of inflicting serious wounds. She often wears a white dress, which may be stained with blood. Some accounts describe her with a gaping hole in her back, through which she sucks the blood of her victims, while others simply state this is where she conceals her entrails. The scent of frangipani flowers often accompanies her presence, masking her true, decaying nature.
habitat: Pontianaks are said to inhabit banana trees, especially those near cemeteries or abandoned houses. They are also believed to frequent forests and other dark, secluded areas. They are often found near bodies of water, such as rivers and swamps.
behavior: The Pontianak is a vengeful and predatory spirit. She lures victims with her beauty and sweet voice, often appearing as a damsel in distress. Once she has gained their trust, she reveals her true form and attacks. She is particularly drawn to men who are traveling alone or who have disrespected women. She is also said to be attracted to the scent of newborn babies. She enjoys tormenting her victims before killing them, often playing with them like a cat with a mouse.

keyMyths.title: The Origin of the Pontianak
keyMyths.description: A woman dies during childbirth, becoming a vengeful spirit due to the trauma and lack of proper burial rites. She seeks retribution on those who wronged her or disrespect women.
keyMyths.source: Various oral traditions across Malaysia and Indonesia

keyMyths.title: The Frangipani Warning
keyMyths.description: The strong scent of frangipani flowers often precedes the arrival of a Pontianak, serving as a warning sign to those who are aware of her presence. Ignoring this sign can be fatal.

sources.author: Rosemary Ellen Guiley
sources.work: The Encyclopedia of Ghosts and Spirits
sources.description: Provides a general overview of the Pontianak and its place in Southeast Asian folklore.
sources.text: Provides a general overview of the Pontianak and its place in Southeast Asian folklore.

sources.author: Various Authors
sources.description: Numerous oral traditions and collected stories contribute to the understanding of Pontianak lore.
sources.text: Numerous oral traditions and collected stories contribute to the understanding of Pontianak lore.
